question: What is a bull put spread?
human_answers: ["Bull means the investor is betting on a rising market. Puts are a type of stock option where the seller of a put option promises to buy 100 shares of stock from the buyer of the put option at a pre-agreed price called the strike price on any day before expiration day.  The buyer of the put option does not have to sell (it is optional, thats why it is called buying an option).  However, the seller of the put is required to make good on their promise to the buyer. The broker can require the seller of the put option to have a deposit, called margin, to help make sure that they can make good on the promise.   Profit... The buyer can profit from the put option if the stock price moves down substantially.   The buyer of the put option does not need to own the stock, he can sell the option to someone else.  If the buyer of the put option also owns the stock, the put option can be thought of like an insurance policy on the value of the stock.  The seller of the put option profits if the stock price stays the same or rises.  Basically, the seller comes out best if they can sell put options that no one ends up using by expiration day. A spread is an investment consisting of buying one option and selling another. Let's put bull and put and spread together with an example from Apple. So, if you believed Apple Inc. AAPL (currently 595.32) was going up or staying the same through JAN you could sell the 600 JAN put and buy the 550 put.  If the price rises beyond 600, your profit would be the difference in price of the puts.  Let's explore this a little deeper (prices from google finance 31 Oct 2012): Worst Case:  AAPL drops below 550.  The bull put spread investor owes (600-550)x100 shares = $5000 in JAN but received $2,035 for taking this risk. EDIT 2016: The \"worst case\" was the outcome in this example, the AAPL stock price on options expiry Jan 18, 2013 was about $500/share. Net profit = $2,035 - $5,000 = -$2965 = LOSS of $2965 Best Case:  AAPL stays above 600 on expiration day in JAN. Net Profit = $2,035 - 0 = $2035 Break Even:  If AAPL drops to 579.65, the value of the 600 JAN AAPL put sold will equal the $2,035 collected and the bull put spread investor will break even. Commissions have been ignored in this example."]
chatgpt_answers:
["A bull put spread is an options strategy with a bullish tilt that involves two puts on the same underlying asset: the trader purchases a put at a lower strike price and sells a put at a higher strike price. It’s called a “bull” spread because it is based on the view that the underlying asset will move upward.

The goal is to profit from the difference in the two put premiums, with the position designed to work best if the underlying asset rises. If that happens, the lower-strike put should decline in value, while the higher-strike put should increase in value, giving the trader a chance to make money by selling the higher-strike put for more than it cost to buy the lower-strike put.

This strategy also has limited risk. The most that can be lost is the gap between the two strike prices minus the premium received from selling the higher-strike put. Put another way, the trader’s downside is limited to the amount paid for the lower-strike put.", "A bull put spread is a bullish options trade where you purchase a put option at a lower strike price and simultaneously sell a put option at a higher strike price on the same underlying asset. Because both positions are entered at the same time, the strategy is designed to profit when the underlying asset increases in price.

For instance:
You buy a put with a $50 strike for $2 per share.
You sell a put with a $55 strike for $1 per share.
Your net cost for the spread is the difference between those amounts, which works out to $1 per share.

When the underlying asset rises, both puts decline in value. The outcome depends on how the spread changes from what you paid for it. So if the underlying asset moves up to $60 per share, the $50 put might drop to $0.50 per share, while the $55 put could become worthless at $0. In that situation, the profit would be $0.50 - $1.00 = -$0.50 per share.

Bull put spreads are commonly used to generate income while still giving the trader exposure to a price increase in the underlying asset. However, the maximum upside is limited to the gap between the two strike prices minus the spread’s cost. If the underlying asset does not rise, or falls significantly, the trader can incur a loss."]

question: Buying from an aggressive salesperson
human_answers: ["If something in any transaction in life—financial or otherwise—doesn’t make you feel comfortable and the choice is between saving money with one thing versus another, don’t sell your personal needs short. Pay more elsewhere that treats you the way you expect to be treated. In the long run the $$$ you “save” in a cheaper transaction might cost you more in the headaches and annoyance you have to swallow in dealing with this “bargain” in the future. Your question is this: “Do his sales tactics indicate other underlying problems? How can I deal effectively with those tactics?” And you state this as well: “To make a long story short, the dealer's aggressive sales tactics have made me somewhat uncomfortable.” And finally ask: “How can I deal effectively with those tactics?” Okay, first and foremost if you feel discomfort in anything in life—not just a financial situation—just walk away. You might have to say “No…” when doing this but it’s not always the case you will have to counter aggression with aggression. And specifically in the case of a purchase like this, you need to also ask yourself: “Is this discount being offered me worth the headache I am getting?” At the end of the day money is meaningless and has it’s main worth as an economic motivator/stimulator: Someone has a need and someone else has something that can solve that need. What would it take for the side of need to connect to the side of solution to that need? This is the basic concept surrounding all economics. So that said, I have personally avoided buying things for less money and paid slightly more elsewhere for a service experience that made me feel comfortable. At the end of the day, if you feel happy in the transaction it helps in the long run more than—let’s say—the $20 to $40 you “save” by buying from someone else. Also—on the side of customer service—this person’s sales techniques sound like something out of a very old fashioned sales playbook. Nowadays it’s all about relationships and service: The immediate sale is not as important for competent and reputable businesses because they know a better customer service experience will bring people back. So it doesn’t matter how long this guy has been in business: It could be that he’s been in business a long time just because he has been in business a long time. That said—and in the case of musical instruments—maybe this guy is really good at care and upkeep of instruments but has crappy sales techniques. Keep that in mind as well and just push back on their sales methods. For things like musical instruments, people might be jerks on the sales side but in the maintenance and repair side they are great. Will you need to go to them if/when your instrument needs repair? Or you don’t care? At the end of the day, go with your gut. And if your gut says, “No…” then just go somewhere else and spend your money on an item you like from a place that treats you the way you need."]

question: Can I add PMI to my principal balance when I take out a mortgage?
human_answers: ["There are few different types of MI you can choose from, they are: Borrower-Paid Monthly (this is what most people think of when they think MI) Borrower-Paid Single Premium (you may have QM issues on this) Lender Paid Single Premium Split Up-front and Monthly The only way to determine which option will ultimately cost you less is to come up with a time estimate or range for how long you anticipate you will hold this mortgage, then look at each option over that time, and see where they fall. To answer your question about the single-premium being added to your loan, this typically does not happen (outside of FHA/VA).  The reason for that is you would now have 90%+ financing and fall into a new pricing bracket, if not being disqualified altogether.  What is far more typical is the use of premium pricing to pay this up-front premium.  Premium pricing is where you take a lender credit in exchange for an elevated rate; it is the exact opposite of paying points to buy down your rate. For example:  say a zero point rate is 4.25%, and you have monthly MI of say .8%.  Your effective rate would be 5.05%.  It may be possible to use premium pricing at an elevated rate of say 4.75% to pay your MI up front--now your effective rate is the note rate of 4.75%.  This is how a single premium can save you money.  Keep in mind though, the 4.75% will be your rate for the life of the loan, and in the other scenario, once the MI drops off, the effective rate will go back down from 5.05% to 4.25%.  This is why it is critical to know your estimated length of financing."]

["Private mortgage insurance, or PMI, is typically required by lenders when the buyer makes a down payment of less than 20% of the home’s purchase price. It serves to shield the lender if the borrower falls behind on mortgage payments. It cannot be added to the mortgage principal.

If your loan includes PMI, make sure to factor those premiums into your budget when estimating how much you can truly afford to borrow."]

question: What forces cause a company to write down goodwill?
human_answers: ["To understand the answer we first have to understand what Goodwill is. Goodwill in a companies balance sheet is an intangible asset that represents the extra value because of a strong brand name, good customer relations, good employee relations and any patents or proprietary technology. An article from The Economist explains this very well and actually talks about Time Warner directly - The goodwill, the bad and the ugly When one firm buys another, the target’s goodwill—essentially the   premium paid over its book value—is added to the combined entity’s   balance-sheet. Goodwill and other intangibles on the books of   companies in the S&P 500 are valued at $2.6 trillion, or 10% of their   total assets, according to analysts at Goldman Sachs. As the economy deteriorates and more firms trade down towards (or even   below) their book value, empire-builders are having to mark down the   value of assets they splashed out on in rosier times. A recently   announced $25 billion goodwill charge is expected to push Time Warner   into an operating loss for 2008, for instance. Michael Moran of   Goldman Sachs thinks such hits could amount to $200 billion or more   over the cycle. Investors have so far paid little attention to   intangibles, but as write-downs proliferate they are likely to become   increasingly wary of industries with a high ratio of goodwill to   assets, such as health care, consumer goods and telecoms. How bad things get will depend on the beancounters. American firms   used to be allowed to amortise goodwill over many years. Since 2002,   when an accounting-rule change ended that practice, goodwill has had   to be tested every year for impairment. In this stormy environment,   with auditors keener than ever to avoid being seen to go easy on   clients, companies are being told to mark down assets if there is any   doubt about their value. The sanguine point out that this has no effect on cashflow, since such   charges are non-cash items. Moreover, some investors take goodwill   write-offs with a pinch of salt, preferring to look past such   non-recurring costs and accept the higher “normalised” earnings   numbers to which managers understandably cling. The largest companies   are thus able to survive thumping blows that might otherwise floor   them, such as the $99 billion loss that the newly formed but   ill-conceived AOL Time Warner, as it then was, reported for 2002. But   the impact can be all too real, as write-downs reduce overall book   value and increase leverage ratios, a particular concern in these   debt-averse times."]
chatgpt_answers:
["Goodwill is an intangible asset representing the premium paid for a business over the fair market value of its tangible assets and liabilities. It typically appears on the balance sheet when one company acquires another for more than the fair market value of its net tangible assets.

A goodwill write-down can result from several forces, including:

Impairment: When the fair market value of a company’s assets and liabilities drops significantly, the goodwill carrying amount may need to be lowered. This can be driven by changes in market conditions, industry developments, or a decline in the company’s financial performance.

Restructuring: Events like a divestiture or merger may trigger a write-down if they lead to a major shift in the fair market value of the company’s assets and liabilities.

Reassessment: Goodwill has to be tested every year to make sure it is still stated correctly. If that annual review indicates the value has changed, a write-down may be required.

Changes in accounting standards: New accounting rules can also force goodwill to be written down. For example, revised standards may require different valuation approaches, which could reduce the reported amount."]

question: How does 83b election work when paying fair market value at time of grant?
human_answers: ["The tax cost at election should be zero.  The appreciation is all capital gain beyond your basis, which will be the value at election. IRC §83 applies to property received as compensation for services, where the property is still subject to a substantial risk of forfeiture.  It will catch unvested equity given to employees. §83(a) stops taxation until the substantial risk of forfeiture abates (i.e. no tax until stock vests) since the item is revocable and not yet truly income.  §83(b) allows the taxpayer to make a quick election (up to 30 days after transfer - firm deadline!) to waive the substantial risk of forfeiture (e.g. treat shares as vested today). The normal operation of §83 takes over after election and the taxable income is generally the value of the vested property minus the price paid for it.  If you paid fair market value today, then the difference is zero and your income from the shares is zero. The shares are now yours for tax purposes, though not for legal purposes.  That means they are most likely a capital asset in your hands, like other stocks you own or trade.  The shares will not be treated as compensation income on vesting, and vesting is not a tax matter for elected shares. If you sell them, you get capital gain (with tax dependent on your holding period) over a basis equal to FMV at the election.  The appreciation past election-FMV will be capital gain, rather than ordinary income.  This is why the §83(b) election is so valuable.  It does not matter at this point whether you bought the restricted shares at FMV or at discount (or received them free) - that only affects the taxes upon §83(b) election."]
chatgpt_answers:
["An 83(b) election is a tax option for someone who receives restricted stock. It allows the recipient to include the stock’s fair market value in taxable income at the time the shares are granted, rather than waiting until the restrictions lapse. Using the election can reduce, or sometimes eliminate, the tax that would otherwise be owed when the stock vests and the restrictions are lifted.

For the election to count, a written statement must be filed with the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) within 30 days after the restricted stock is received. The statement needs to include the person’s name and address, the company’s name and address, a description of the stock, and the date the stock was transferred.

If the recipient pays fair market value at grant, that payment is treated as taxable compensation. In that situation, the amount paid has to be included in taxable income in the year the stock is received, regardless of whether an 83(b) election is made."]

question: Can a retail trader do bid-ask spread scalping through algo-trading?
human_answers: ["In US public stock markets there is no difference between the actions individual retail traders are \"permitted\" to take and the actions institutional/corporate traders are \"permitted\" to take.  The only difference is the cost of those actions.  For example, if you become a Registered Market Maker on, say, the BATS stock exchange, you'll get some amazing rebates and reduced transaction prices; however, in order to qualify for Registered Market Maker status you have to maintain constant orders in the book for hundreds of equities at significant volumes.  An individual retail trader is certainly permitted to do that, but it's probably too expensive. Algorithmic trading is not the same as automated trading (algorithmic trading can be non-automated, and automated trading can be non-algorithmic), and both can be anywhere from low- to high-frequency.  A low-frequency automated strategy is essentially indistinguishable from a person clicking their mouse several times per day, so: no, from a legal or regulatory perspective there is no special procedure an individual retail trader has to follow before s/he can automate a trading strategy.  (Your broker, on the other hand, may have all sorts of hoops for you to jump through in order to use their automation platform.) Last (but certainly not least) you will almost certainly lose money hand over fist attempting bid-ask scalping as an individual retail trader, whether your approach is algorithmic or not, automated or not.  Why?  Because the only way to succeed at bid-ask scalping is to (a) always be at/near the front of the queue when a price change occurs in your favor, and (b) always cancel your resting orders before they are executed when a price change occurs against you.  Unless your algorithms are smarter than every other algorithm in the industry, an individual retail trader operating through a broker's trading platform cannot react quickly enough to succeed at either of those.  You would have to eschew the broker and buy direct market access to even have a chance, and that's the point at which you're no longer a retail trader. Good luck!"]
chatgpt_answers: ["Yes, a retail trader can use algo-trading to scalp the bid-ask spread. Scalping is a tactic focused on taking very small price changes and turning them into profit. In the context of the bid-ask spread, that means buying at the ask and then quickly selling at the bid, hoping to capture the difference between the two prices. Algorithmic trading can handle this process automatically, allowing the trader to place many orders in a short period without manually entering each one. Even so, scalping is a high-frequency method that requires a strong appetite for risk and the ability to react quickly to shifting market conditions. It is not suitable for every trader."]

question: What is high trading volume in a stock indicative of? Is high liquidity a good thing or a bad thing?

question: How to calculate the number of months until a loan is paid off (given principal, APR and payment amount)?
human_answers: ["Here is the derivation of the formula, with The loan is equal to the sum of the repayments discounted to present value."]
chatgpt_answers:
["To work out how many months it will take to pay off a loan, use this formula:

number of months = (principal * (APR / 12)) / (payment amount - (APR / 12))

Where:
principal = the loan’s starting balance, in dollars
APR = the annual interest rate, written as a decimal
payment amount = the fixed monthly payment, in dollars

This assumes the loan has a constant monthly payment and a fixed APR.

For instance, if you borrow $10,000 at an APR of 0.05 (5%) and pay $300 each month, the calculation looks like this:

number of months = ($10,000 * (0.05 / 12)) / ($300 - (0.05 / 12))

This works out to roughly 41.7 months.

Note: this is only an approximation, because it does not account for any extra fees or charges that may be added to the loan."]

question: How do small cap stocks perform vs. large cap stocks (like Dow constituents) during bear trends?

That being said, small cap businesses will, in general, likely do worse than large cap stocks, for several reasons. This was/is certainly the case in the Great Recession.  Small cap businesses have, on average, higher betas, which is a measure of a company's risk compared to the overall market. This means that small cap companies, on average outperform large cap companies during boom times, but it also means that they suffer more on average during bear times. The debate over whether or not the standard beta is still useful for small cap companies continues, however. Some economists feel that small cap companies are better measured against the Russell 2000 or similar indexes instead of the S&P 500.  Small cap companies may face problems accessing or maintaining access to lines of credit. During the Great Recession, major lenders decreased their lending to small businesses, which might make it harder for them to weather the storm.  On a related point, small businesses might not have as large an asset base to use as collateral for loans in bad times. One notable large cap company that used its asset base to their advantage was Ford, which gave banks partial ownership of its factories during hard times. This a) gave Ford a good amount of cash with which to continue their short-term operations, and b) gave the banks a vested interest in keeping Ford's lines of credit open. Ford struggled, but it never faced the financial problems of GM and Chrysler. question: Why could rental costs for apartments/houses rise while buying prices can go up and down?
human_answers: ["Economically, you would say that purchased and rented real estate are not perfect substitutes--they are largely separate markets.  Only a few people are able to easily switch from one to the other and that choice is sticky--for example, once you buy a house, prices would have to rise a lot for it to be worth it to sell it and move into an apartment. In both markets there is a supply and demand curve, but the slope of the demand curve for houses to purchase is much steeper than the demand curve for rentals. The market for new housing fluctuates rapidly because it requires a large change in housing prices to change the number of people looking to buy a house.  Most decisions to buy a house are not driven by the state of the housing market.  This describes a supply/demand graph with a very steep demand curve.  Additionally, because of the leverage provided by mortgages, the demand for houses depends critically on relatively small changes in the interest rate and availability of loans.  Thus the steep demand curve shifts all over the place as borrowing conditions change. On the other hand, apartment prices are more stable because people easily move from one apartment to another and people living in their parent's basements easily move into apartments if prices change.  A small change in the price or quantity of rentals brings about reasonable response in quantity demanded.  This is the situation where the demand curve is shallow.  In addition, rentals are not tied to interest rates tightly, nor are they as strongly tied to economic conditions (in a recession, people avoid buying but renters continue to rent)."]
chatgpt_answers:
["Rent and home-sale prices are driven by different sets of forces. A major factor behind rent is how many people want to lease in a particular area: when demand is high, landlords can often push rents up because tenants are competing for a limited number of units. When demand is softer, they may need to lower rents to keep vacancies down.

Rents can also increase if the expense of owning and operating a rental property rises. Bigger property taxes, higher utility costs, and more expensive repairs can all lead landlords to charge more in order to cover those costs.

By contrast, house prices usually fluctuate with a wider mix of influences, including:

The overall housing market: If buyer demand is strong and inventory is tight, prices often rise. If demand weakens or there are many homes on the market, prices can fall.

Interest rates: When rates are lower, borrowing becomes cheaper, which can draw in more buyers and support higher prices. When rates rise, mortgages cost more, demand can cool, and prices can come under pressure.

Economic conditions: A strong economy typically boosts housing demand and can lift prices. In a weaker economy, demand may ease and prices can decline.

Location: Certain cities or neighborhoods are more desirable, so homes there tend to sell for more. Factors like school quality, job prospects, and overall neighborhood appeal all matter.

That’s why rental costs and purchase prices can react differently: they’re influenced by related but not identical pressures, so rents may keep climbing even as home prices move both up and down."]

question: Problems with Enterprise Value and better valuation techniques
human_answers: ["This is an example from another field, real estate. Suppose you buy a $100,000 house with a 20 percent down payment, or $20,000, and borrow the other $80,000. In this example, your \"equity\" or \"market cap\" is $20,000. But the total value, or \"enterprise value\" of the house, is actually $100,000, counting the $80,000 mortgage.  \"Enterprise value\" is what a buyer would have to pay to own the company or the house \"free and clear,\" counting the debt."]
chatgpt_answers:
["Enterprise value, or EV, estimates a company’s value by taking market capitalization and then adding debt, minority interests, and preferred stock. It is often preferred over market cap because it provides a more complete picture of the company’s financial structure.

Even so, EV has important shortcomings as a valuation measure. It does not account for cash and cash equivalents, despite the fact that these balances can be significant. Because EV includes debt but excludes cash, a company with a larger EV may actually carry less net debt than another company with a smaller EV.

EV also omits the cost of capital, which is the return investors expect on their investment. That means a business with a higher EV can still be less attractive than one with a lower EV if its cost of capital is higher.

A further weakness is that EV does not incorporate growth expectations, even though growth can be a major source of value. A company with better projected growth may deserve a higher valuation than a similar firm with the same EV but a weaker outlook.

Other valuation methods attempt to capture some of these missing elements. Discounted cash flow, or DCF, is widely used because it considers future cash flows and the cost of capital. Net asset value, or NAV, is another approach that looks at net assets like cash, investments, and tangible assets.

Since no single valuation method is flawless, it is generally better to use several approaches together to form a more balanced view of a company’s worth."]

question: How does a limit order work for a credit spread?
human_answers: ["As you probably know, a credit spread involves buying a call (or put) at one strike and selling another call (or put) at another with the same maturity, so you're dealing with two orders. Your broker will likely have to fill this order themselves, meaning that they'll have to look at the existing bid/asks for the different strikes and wait until the difference matches (or exceeds) your limit order.  Obviously they can't place limit orders on the legs individually since they can't guarantee that they will both be executed.  They also don't care what the individual prices are; they just care what the difference is.  It's possible that they have computer systems that examine existing bids and asks that would fill your order, but it's still done by the broker, not the exchange.  The exchange never sees your actual limit order; they will just see the market orders placed by your broker."]
chatgpt_answers:
["A limit order tells the market to buy or sell an instrument at a specified price or better. In the case of a credit spread, it allows you to set the maximum price you’re willing to pay for the spread, or the minimum price you’re prepared to accept when selling it.

For example, if you’re considering a credit spread trade that involves buying a call option and selling a put option, you might enter a limit order to buy the spread at a given price or lower. Put another way, you’re indicating that you won’t pay more than that amount.

If the spread’s market price falls below your limit, the order is executed and the trade is completed. If the price never reaches your limit, the order stays open and the trade doesn’t happen.

In general, limit orders are useful in credit spread trading because they give you control over the entry price. That can help you avoid overpaying for the spread and may reduce potential losses."]

question: How does conversion of Secured Convertible Notes work?
human_answers: ["Let's assume that the bonds have a par value of $1,000.  If conversion happens, then one bond would be converted into 500 shares.  The price in the market is unimportant.  Regardless of the share price in the market, the income per share would be increased by the absence of $70 in interest expense.  It would be decreased by the lost tax deduction.  It would be further diluted by the increase in 500 shares.  Likewise, the debt would be extinguished and the equity section increased.  Whether it increased or decreased on a per share basis would depend upon the average amount paid in per share in the currently existing structure, adjusted for changes in retained earnings since the initial offering and for any treasury shares. There would be a loss in value, generally, if it is trading far from $2.00 because it would be valued based on the market price.  Had the bond not converted, it would trade in the market as a pure bond if the stock price is far below the strike price and as an ordinary pure bond plus a premium if near enough to the strike price in a manner that depends upon the time remaining under the conversion privilege.   I cannot think of a general case where someone would want to convert below strike and indeed, barring a very strange tax, inheritance or legal situation (such as a weird divorce), I cannot think of a case where it would make sense.  It often does not make sense to convert far from maturity either as the option premium only vanishes well above $2.  The primary case for conversion would be where the after-tax dividend is greater than the after-tax interest payment."]

["Secured convertible notes (SCNs) are debt instruments that can later be exchanged for equity, typically once certain conditions are met. If the investor opts not to convert, the notes typically mature at the end of the fixed term, at which point the investor receives the original principal plus any accrued interest.

SCN terms can vary significantly from one issuer to another, so it’s important to examine the specific note carefully before investing."]

question: What are the typical repayment plans for Credit Cards in the United States?

Fixed payment plan: With this method, you pay the same amount each month, typically above the minimum. Balance transfer plan: This means shifting the debt from one credit card to another card with a lower interest rate. It can cut interest charges and help you pay down the balance faster. question: Buying a home with down payment from family as a “loan”
human_answers: ["I'll compare it to a situation that is different, but will involve the same cash flow.  Imagine the buyer agrees that you buy only 70% of the house right now, and the remaining 30% in 7 years time.  It would be obviously fair to pay 70% of today's value today, pay 30% of a reasonable rent for 7 years (because 30% of the house isn't owned by you), then pay 30% of the value that the house has in 7 years time. 30% of the value in 7 years is the same as 30% of the value today, plus 30% of whatever the house gained in value.  Instead you pay 70% of today's value, you pay no rent for the 30% that you don't own, then in 7 years time you pay 30% of today's value, plus 50% of whatever the house gained in value. So you are basically exchanging 30% of seven years rent, plus interest, for 20% of the gain in value over 7 years. Which might be zero. Or might be very little. Or a lot, in which case you are still better off.   Obviously you need to set up a bullet proof contract. A lawyer will also tell you what to put into the contract in case the house burns down and can't be rebuilt, or you add an extension to the home which increases the value. Another 401(k) choice may be an S&P 500 index fund. Pros of an S&P 500 index fund:
Diversification: The S&P 500 contains 500 large-cap stocks, offering exposure across a range of sectors and industries.

chatgpt_answers:
["Return on capital is a measure of how well a business converts the capital it uses into profit. The simplest version is net income divided by total capital. As a rule, a higher return on capital suggests the company is more efficient at generating profits from the capital available to it.

Joel Greenblatt, an American hedge fund manager and author, developed a stock-selection method he called the “magic formula” to identify undervalued companies. It evaluates businesses with two key metrics:

Return on capital (ROC): This is calculated as earnings before interest and taxes (EBIT) divided by invested capital (IC). A larger ROC means the company is putting its capital to more productive use in producing profits.

Earnings yield (EY): This is calculated as EBIT divided by enterprise value (EV). A higher earnings yield points to a company that may be more undervalued.

Under the magic formula, you compute ROC and EY for each company and then rank them by both measures. The companies that score best on ROC and also have the highest EY rise to the top.

The idea is that firms that are both capital-efficient and cheap relative to value may produce above-average returns. Even so, this is just one way to look for undervalued stocks, and there is no guarantee it will outperform other approaches. As with any investment decision, it’s important to weigh the risks and possible rewards before moving ahead."]

question: Do governments support their own bonds when their value goes down?
human_answers: ["Without getting to hung-up on terminology here, the management of a company will often attempt to keep stock prices high because of a number of reasons: Ideally companies keep prices up through performance.  In some cases, you'll see companies do other things spending cash and/or issuing bonds to continue to pay dividends (e.g. IBM), or spending cash and/or issuing bonds to pay for stock buybacks (e.g. IBM).  These methods can work for a time but are not sustainable and will often be seen as acts of desperation.  Companies that have a solid plan for growth will typically not do much of anything to directly change stock prices. Bonds are a bit different because they have a fairly straight-forward valuation model based on the fact that they pay out a fixed amount per month.  The two main reason prices in bonds go down are: The key here is that bonds pay out the same thing per month regardless of their price or the price of other bonds available.  Most stocks do not pay any dividend and for much of those that do, the main factor as to whether you make or lose money on them is the stock price. The price of bonds does matter to governments, however.  Let's say a country successfully issued some 10 year bonds last year at the price of 1000.  They pay 1% per month (to keep the math simple.)  Every month, they pay out $10 per bond.  Then some (stupid) politicians start threatening to default on bond payments.  The bond market freaks and people start trying to unload these bonds as fast as they can.  The going price drops to $500.  Next month, the payments are the same.  The coupon rate on the bonds has not changed at all.  I'm oversimplifying here but this is the core of how bond prices work. You might be tempted to think that doesn't matter to the country but it does.  Now, this same country wants to issue some more bonds.  It wants to get that 1% rate again but it can't.  Why would anyone pay $1000 for a 1% (per month) bond when they can get the exact same bond with (basically) the same risks for $500?  Instead they have to offer a 2% (per month) rate in order to match the market price. A government (or company) could in fact put money into the bond market to bolster the price of it's bonds (i.e. keep the rates down.)  The problem is that if you are issuing bonds, it's generally (caveats apply) because you need cash that you don't have so what money are you going to use to buy these bonds?  Or in other words, it doesn't make sense to issue bonds and then simply plow the cash gained from that issuance back into the same bonds you are issuing.  The options here are a bit more limited.  I have to mention though that the US government (via a quasi-governmental entity) did actually buy it's own bonds.  This policy of Quantitative Easing (QE) was done for more complicated reasons than simply keeping the price of bonds up."]

question: How to understand the caculation of interest for credit cards?
human_answers: ["Suppose you have been paying interest on previous charges in the past. Your monthly statement is issued on April 12, and (since you just received your income tax refund), you pay it off in full on April 30.  You don't charge anything to the card at all after April 12.  Thus, on April 30, your credit card balance  shows as zero since you just paid it off.  But your April 12 statement billed you for interest only till April 12.  So, on May 12, your next monthly bill will be for the interest for your nonzero balance from April 13 through April 30. Assuming that you still are not making any new charges on your card and pay off the May 12 bill in timely fashion, you will finally have a zero bill on June 12. What if you charge new items to your credit card after April 12? Well, your balance stopped revolving on April 30, and that's when interest is no longer charged on the new charges. But you do owe interest for a charge on April 13 (say) until April 30 when your balance is no longer revolving, and this will be added to your bill on May 12. Purchases made after April 30 will not be charged interest unless you fall off the wagon again and don't pay your May 12 bill in full by the due date of the bill (some time in early June)."]
chatgpt_answers:
["Credit card interest is generally calculated with a daily or monthly periodic rate, then applied to whatever balance remains unpaid. That periodic rate is simply the APR converted into a smaller time unit; APR is the yearly rate listed for the cardholder.

For example, if a card has a 15% APR and interest is charged monthly, the periodic rate would be 1.25% (15% divided by 12 months). If the balance is $1,000 and interest is calculated daily, the daily periodic rate would be 0.00410959% (1.25% divided by 30 days).

The interest on the balance is found by multiplying the periodic rate by the amount owed. So with a $1,000 balance and a daily periodic rate of 0.00410959%, the interest for that day would be $0.41 (0.00410959% × $1,000).

Also, not every card issuer uses the same method, and some charge different rates on certain transactions, such as cash advances or balance transfers. It’s a good idea to read your card’s terms and conditions carefully so you know exactly how interest is assessed and what may change the rate."]

For options, long convexity means being the buyer of the option. question: Investing: P/E Ratio basic question
human_answers: ["While on the surface it might not make sense to pay more than one dollar to get just one dollar back, the key thing is that a good company's earnings are recurring each year. So, you wouldn't just be paying for the $1 dollar of earnings per share this year, but for the entire future stream of earnings per share, every year, in perpetuity -- and the earnings may grow over time too (if it remains a good company.) Your stock is a claim on a portion of the company's future. The brighter and/or more certain that future, the more investors are willing to pay for each recurring dollar of earnings. And the P/E ratio tells you, in effect, how many years it might take for your investment to earn back what you paid – assuming earnings remain the same. But you would hope the earnings would grow, too. When a company's earnings are widely expected to grow, the P/E for the stock is often higher than average. Bear in mind you don't actually receive the company's earnings, since management often decides to reinvest all or a portion of it to grow the company. Yet, many companies do pay a portion of earnings out as dividends. Dividends are money in your pocket each year."]
chatgpt_answers: ["The price-to-earnings ratio, commonly called the P/E ratio, is a financial metric used to gauge a stock’s relative valuation. To work it out, divide the current market price per share by the company’s earnings per share (EPS) from the past 12 months. Investors often use the P/E ratio to compare valuations among companies in the same industry or to measure a company against the overall market average. A higher P/E can indicate that a stock is expensive, while a lower P/E may point to undervaluation. Still, the P/E ratio is only one part of the picture when evaluating a stock. A company’s growth outlook, financial health, and industry conditions can all affect how it should be valued. As always, it’s sensible to research carefully and consider multiple factors before making any investment decisions."]

question: How does a high share price benefit a company when it is raising funds?
human_answers: ["Share price is based on demand.  Assuming the same amount of shares are made available for trade then stocks with a higher demand will have a higher price.  So say a company has 1000 shares in total and that company needs to raise $100. They decide to sell 100 shares for $1 to raise their $100. If there is demand for 100 shares for at least $1 then they achieve their goal. But if the market decides the shares in this company are only worth 50 cents then the company only raises $50. So where do they get the other $50 they needed? Well one option is to sell another 100 shares. The dilution comes about because in the first scenario the company retains ownership of 900 or 90% of the equity. In the second scenario it retains ownership of only 800 shares or 80% of the equity. The benefit to the company and shareholders of a higher price is basically just math. Any multiple of shares times a higher price means there is more value to owning those shares. Therefore they can sell fewer shares to raise the same amount.  A lot of starts up offer employees shares as part of their remuneration package because cash flow is typically tight when starting a new business. So if you're trying to attract the best and brightest it's easier to offer them shares if they are worth more than those of company with a similar opportunity down the road. Share price can also act as something of a credit score. In that a higher share price \"may\" reflect a more credit worthy company and therefore \"may\" make it easier for that company to obtain credit. All else being equal, it also makes it more expensive for a competitor to take over a company the higher the share price. So it can offer some defensive and offensive advantages. All ceteris paribus of course."]

question: How will interest rate changes affect my government bonds ETF?
human_answers: ["In general, yes. If interest rates go higher, then any existing fixed-rate bonds - and hence ETFs holding those bonds - become less valuable. The further each bond is from maturity, the larger the impact. As you suggest, once the bonds do mature, the fund can replace them at a market price, so the effect tails off. The bond market has a concept known as \"duration\" that helps reason about this effect. Roughly, it measures the average time from now to each payout of the bond, weighted by the payout. The longer the duration, the more the price will change for a given change in interest rates. The concept is just an approximation, and there are various slightly different ways of calculating it; but very roughly the price of a bond will reduce by a percentage equal to the duration times the increase in interest rates. So a bond with a duration of 5 years will lose 5% of its value for a 1% rise in interest rates (and of course vice-versa). For your second question, it really depends on what you're trying to achieve by diversifying - this might be best as a different question that gives more detail, as it's not very related to your first question. Short-term bonds are less risky. But both will lose value if the underlying company is in trouble. Gilts (government bonds) are less risky than corporate bonds."]

question: Could ignoring sunk costs be used to make an investment look more attractive when it's really not?
human_answers: ["I'm not sure that you're considering all the options.   So you may not subtract $X from B, but you do compare NPV(B) to $Y.   Also, remember that we're not trying to figure out the return on B.  We're trying to figure out what to do next.  In terms of planning, the sunk cost is irrelevant.  But in terms of calculating return, A was a turkey.  And to calculate the return, we would include $X in our costs for B.  And for the second option, we'd subtract $X from $Y (may be negative).   Sunk costs are irrelevant to planning, but they are very relevant to retrospective analysis.  Please don't confuse the two.  When looking back, part of the cost for B will be that $X.  But in the middle, after paying $X and before starting B, the $X is gone.  You only have the building and have to make your decision based on the options you have at that moment.   You will sometimes hear $Y called the opportunity cost of B.  You could sell out for $Y or you could do B.  You should only do B if it is worth more than $Y.   The sunk cost fallacy would be comparing B to $X.  Assuming $Y is less than $X, this would make you not do B when it is your best path forward from that moment.  I.e. $Y < NPV(B) < $X means that you should do the project.  You will lose money (apparently that's a foregone conclusion), but you will lose less money than if you just sold out.   You should also do B if $Y < $X < NPV(B) or $X < $Y < NPV(B).  In general, you should do B any time $Y < NPV(B).  The only time you should not do B is if NPV(B) < $Y.  If they are exactly equal, then it doesn't matter financially whether you do B or not."]
chatgpt_answers:
["Yes, leaving sunk costs out can make an investment appear more attractive, but that is generally a poor way to evaluate it. Sunk costs are amounts that have already been spent and cannot be recovered. When they are ignored, money already committed to the investment is effectively omitted from the decision about whether to keep funding it.

That omission may improve the investment’s appearance in the short term, but it often produces worse choices over time. Sunk costs are not supposed to influence the decision to continue or abandon an investment; that choice should depend on the investment’s expected future returns.

For example, if you have already invested $10,000 in a company that is performing badly, brushing aside that sunk $10,000 and putting in more money because you expect a turnaround could end up costing you even more if the company never recovers.

In general, investment decisions should be made using all relevant information, including both prior spending and the likely future payoff. Ignoring sunk costs can skew the analysis and result in further losses."]

question: Is there a way to roll over short-term gains and avoid capital gain tax
human_answers: ["If the investments are in a non-retirement, taxable account, there's not much you can do to avoid short-term capital gains if you sell now. A common method is to sell the investment that produced the short-term gain and then put the proceeds into a similar asset within a specified window, typically 60 days. This is often called a “tax-free rollover” or a “like-kind exchange.”

To qualify, the asset you dispose of and the one you buy must be similar, or “like-kind.” For example, you might sell stocks and use the money to purchase bonds, or sell one rental property and acquire another rental property. What you cannot do is sell stocks and then use the proceeds to buy a car or another personal-use asset.

It’s important to note that these rollovers apply only to investments held for less than one year. After an asset has been held for more than a year, any gain is treated as a long-term capital gain, which is taxed at a lower rate than a short-term gain.

question: What causes thrombocytopenia?I'm 17 years old (150 lbs, 5'6") and was diagnosed with thrombocytopenia about two weeks ago.  I'm also suffering from mild depression. chatgpt_answers:
["Thrombocytopenia means there are too few platelets in the blood. Platelets are the small blood cells that help the body make clots after an injury. A normal platelet count is roughly 150,000 to 450,000 platelets per microliter of blood. Once the count drops below 150,000, it is considered low and bleeding risk can increase.

There are many possible causes of thrombocytopenia, including:

Infections: viral or bacterial infections can decrease platelet production or cause platelets to be destroyed more quickly.

Medications: certain drugs, such as heparin, aspirin, and nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), may lead to thrombocytopenia.

Autoimmune disorders: illnesses like lupus or rheumatoid arthritis can cause the immune system to produce antibodies that attack platelets.

Cancer: some cancers, including leukemia and lymphoma, can reduce platelet counts by disrupting platelet production or increasing platelet breakdown.

Nutritional deficiency: not having enough nutrients such as iron or folic acid can contribute to a low platelet count.

Pregnancy: in some pregnant women, thrombocytopenia develops because the body’s platelet demand rises during pregnancy.

Alcohol abuse: heavy alcohol use can also lower platelet counts.

It’s important to keep working with your healthcare provider to identify the exact cause of the low platelet count and get appropriate treatment. In certain cases, vitamin or mineral supplements may help increase platelets, but they should only be used under the guidance of a healthcare provider."]

A lump just beneath the skull might be caused by:

A cyst: a closed sac that contains fluid, gas, or other material. Cysts can occur in many areas of the body and may feel firm or soft.

A tumor: an abnormal growth of cells. Some tumors are malignant, while others are benign.

An abscess: a collection of pus in the tissue. Abscesses can develop because of infection, injury, or other reasons.

The next step is to have a healthcare provider examine the area and determine what it is. They may perform a physical exam and, if necessary, order tests like imaging or a biopsy.

Until then, avoid squeezing or repeatedly touching the lump, since that could irritate it or worsen a possible infection. If you notice other symptoms such as fever, chills, or difficulty swallowing, be sure to mention them to your healthcare provider."]

question: Q. How to manage nosebleed?Hi doctor,My 3 year old daughter has had three nosebleeds in about 3 hours. During the nosebleeds, she said her cheek bone area close to her nose hurts. What to do?
human_answers: ["Hello. For further information consult a child health specialist online --> https://www.icliniq.com/ask-a-doctor-online/child-health-specialist"]
chatgpt_answers:
["Nosebleeds can be frightening, particularly in a small child, but they are usually not serious and can often be managed at home. For your daughter, try having her sit upright and lean slightly forward. This helps prevent blood from draining into the throat, which can cause nausea or vomiting. Then have her pinch the soft part of her nose, just beneath the bony bridge. That puts pressure on the blood vessels and may help the bleeding stop. Hold the pressure for at least 5–10 minutes.

If it is still bleeding after 10 minutes, you can try a saline nasal spray or apply a thin layer of petroleum jelly inside the nostrils. Both can help keep the nasal lining moist and may reduce the chance of further nosebleeds. If the bleeding continues, or if she keeps getting repeated nosebleeds, contact her healthcare provider. You should also seek advice if she develops other symptoms such as fever, headache, or facial pain.

For the moment, it may help to add moisture to the air in your home with a humidifier, or even a bowl of water placed near a heat source, since dry air can make nosebleeds more likely. Also try to avoid things that can irritate the nose, like nose picking or blowing too forcefully. I hope this is helpful, and if you’re still concerned or have more questions, please reach out to your healthcare provider."]

["Liver function tests are only one piece of the picture when it comes to liver health. They can give clues about how the liver is doing, but they do not establish a diagnosis on their own. The ultrasound result, the child’s symptoms, and his medical history are also important. It’s also difficult to interpret the numbers without the laboratory’s own reference ranges, since these can vary slightly from one lab to another. Normal values can also differ by age and sex.

In broad terms, the tests you listed are used as follows:

Alkaline phosphatase (ALP): an enzyme present in the liver, bones, and small intestine. A high result can be seen with liver or bone problems.  
Alanine transaminase (ALT): a liver enzyme. An elevated level may indicate liver injury or disease.  
Aspartate transaminase (AST): found in the liver, heart, muscle, and other tissues. A raised level can also suggest liver damage or disease.  
Total bilirubin: a breakdown product of red blood cells. If this is high, it may be associated with liver or blood disorders.  
Direct bilirubin: one fraction of bilirubin. When elevated, it can point toward liver disease.  
Gamma-glutamyltransferase (GGT): an enzyme located in the liver, pancreas, and bile ducts. Increased levels may suggest liver or bile duct disease.  
Albumin: a protein produced by the liver. Low levels may be seen in liver disease or poor nutrition.

The best next step is to review these results with your child’s healthcare provider, who can interpret them together with the ultrasound findings and the rest of his clinical situation."]

question: how do forensic auditors examine financial reporting

A few steps are involved in making sedimentary rock:

Deposition: Weathering and erosion break down older rocks and minerals into sediment. Wind, water, or ice then carry that sediment and drop it somewhere new.

The main types of sedimentary rocks include:

Chemical sedimentary rocks: These form when dissolved minerals precipitate out of solution and collect. Rock salt, limestone, and dolomite are examples.

Organic sedimentary rocks: These are formed from gathered organic material, such as shells from marine organisms or plant remains. Coal and limestone are examples.

question: - How do lightbulbs know the correct wattage to draw from the electrical current . How does a 60 watt bulb and 20 watt bulb fit into the same socket yet know to draw more or less amps ? Explain like I'm five.
human_answers: ["Just as your garden hose can have different size nozzles , but the nozzle does n't know how much water to spray , that 's determined by the size of the nozzle . The filament in the light resists electrical current . Different filaments offer different resistances . More resistance means less electrical flow , means less light , means lower wattage .", "There is a mathematical relationship between voltage , amperage , wattage and electrical resistance . To get a 60 watt bulb you have to have a bulb that draws .5454 amps on a 110 volt line ( which is what your house has for lamps ) . That is because watts = amps X volts . So you know how many amps this bulb has to draw so how do you get a bulb to draw .5454 amps ? You add resistance to the circuit ( bulb ) . To get the correct amount of resistance for a .5454 amp 60 watt bulb on a 110 volt line you need 201 ohms of resistance . We know this because resistance = volts / amps .", "Wattage is the measure of energy used per time , expressed as joules per second . Amperage is a measure of how much charge is flowing through the wire , expressed in coulombs per second . In your house , you will have many different devices that all draw different wattages and currents , but they all are made to run on the same voltage ( Joules per coulomb ) , which is 110V in the US and 240V in other places . The wattage of each device is then determined by the voltage that goes to your house and the resistance of each device , using the formula P = V^2 / R. Since all the devices run on the same voltage , they have to be made with different resistances to draw different amounts of power . For your 60 watt bulb , you can get resistance by dividing 110 ^ 2 /60 to get a resistance of 201 Ohms . Once you know the resistance , it 's easy to find the current that it draws using V = I*R ( .54 Amps ) ."]

Both bulbs fit the same socket because the socket provides electricity, and the bulb’s resistor determines how much of that electricity gets used."]

Voltage is like elevation of the waterfall . Current is the amount of water that flows down the waterfall . You may have heard of Ohm 's law before . We write V = I x R , where V is voltage , I is current , and R is is something called resistance . If we have a resistor with a certain value for R , putting a specific voltage across the two terminals of the device means the same current will flow through it every time . Ohm 's law describes what this current is . Memristors are a lot like resistors . They have two terminals . Current flows through them . The difference here is that their resistance changes . The more current that flows through it , the higher the resistance goes . If you reverse the direction the current flows , the resistance gets smaller again . Now how is this useful ? We currently use something called DRAM for computer memory . Memory stores a bunch of ones and zeros . Have you ever pulled some laundry out of the dryer and got shocked ? If they have a high resistance , you could have that mean its storing a 1 . If it has a low resistance , you could have it mean that it is storing a zero . question: Difference between monolithic and micro kernel . Which category does linux belong to ? Explain like I'm five.
human_answers: ["Linux is a monolithic kernel . Every kernel architect has his or her own definition of what constitutes a nano kernel , a micro kernel , a monolithic kernel , and a hybrid kernel . In general , a monolithic kernel has most , if not all of the operating system 's core elements sitting within the kernel and operating with supervisory privileges . Non - executive system services and user programs run with reduced privileges . Executive services and device drivers can communicate with one another rapidly through simple procedure calls ; however , a single poorly written component can break the whole kernel . In a hybrid kernel , some of the operating system 's core elements are removed from the kernel and run with reduced privileges alongside user programs and non - executive system services . In a microkernel , only essential resource management and communication functions sit within the kernel and run with supervisory privilege . Everything else , including executive system services , run with reduced privileges . Executive services must communicate with one through inter - process communication managed by the kernel ; this is much slower than procedure calls but a poorly written component will affect only itself .", "A monolithic kernel runs the operating in \" kernel space \" , a privileged position , and defines all the virtual interfaces over the hardware . When you write a program , you do n't tell the hard drive to open a particular file , let alone how to do it - file - open is an abstraction provided by the OS , whether it 's a hard drive , CD drive , or other device . A microkernel is very small by comparison , and handles few essential services like memory management , and has few generic interfaces which drivers and protocol stacks can interact with . Drivers and protocol stacks are n't run in privileged as the kernel itself is , they 're ran in user space , which has a different set of permissions and access control over the system , dictated by the kernel . User space has to cross logical barriers and get permission to do certain elevated tasks . Monolithic kernels have kernel space vs. user space , too , it 's just with the micro kernel , more is in user space . The advantage is anything in user space can crash or get dumped and replaced without taking down the whole system . In a monolithic kernel , if there is a bug in the file system code , it effects the whole system . Exo kernels are also an exciting branch of kernel design - these perform one role , multiplex the hardware . That 's it . Even memory management is a user space program . This allows these low level services to interact with one another without requiring kernel access ( a computationally expensive context switch ) , and each service can be custom curtailed for the application for higher performance . The Linux kernel is arguable whether it 's a micro kernel or monolithic kernel , it 's neither , not purely , at least . It does a shit ton , lots of essential services and protocols , and their interfaces , are defined by the kernel , but it can also fork that work out to modules in user space .", "An OS with a monolithic kernel has everything piled into a single \" program \" . Technically , your network stack could muck about with the data belonging to the disk access routines ( but they * should n't * ) . A microkernel OS enforces the idea that unrelated parts should n't be able to muck about with each other 's data & resources by making each part a separate \" program \" and requiring them to communicate through a small , message - passing core ( the microkernel ) so that this is the * only * way separate components can interact . All major commercial operating systems these days are monolithic . While , technically , OSX is build on top of a microkernel , it 's only running a single \" service \" that handles everything ."]
chatgpt_answers:
["A monolithic kernel is like one big package of software that does almost everything an operating system needs. It talks to the hardware, runs programs, and provides services for those programs. A microkernel is much smaller: it keeps only the most basic parts required to keep the operating system going, and then relies on other programs, often called servers, to handle extra jobs.

Linux is a monolithic kernel. In other words, the code that runs the operating system is all tied together in one large piece.

An easier way to imagine it:

Picture a house. A monolithic kernel is a house where everything is inside the same building — bedrooms, kitchen, bathroom, living room, all under one roof. A microkernel is more like a very small house that only has the essentials, like walls and a roof, and it depends on separate buildings for things such as the kitchen or bathroom.

Hope that makes it clearer! If you want, I can explain it another way too."]

question: What ’s the difference between horsepower and torque ? So I ’ve been asked to explain this recently and I just realized I ca n’t ! What ’s a good analogy and what would one rather have more of in a high performance car ? Explain like I'm five.
human_answers: ["Enzo Ferrari 's famous quote is ; > Horsepower sells cars , torque wins races . Torque is basically just twisting force ; the higher the torque , the easier it is to change the rate of rotation of the output shaft ( which is connected to the wheels ) , which allows you to accelerate better with load . Ergo , the car that can put out more torque from a dead stop is the car that will accelerate faster , assuming both cars are the same weight . Horsepower is just a measure of power ( functionally no different from Watts , just bigger ) . Power is the product of torque and rotational speed , so you can achieve high horsepower either with high torque and low speeds , or with lower torque and high speeds .", "This is a very easy , down to earth explanation . Torque is the force that actually moves the car , hp is a calculation used to show how effectively you have use that torque with gearing . In * a single gear * , your cars acceleration will follow the torque curve exactly . So if you are in 1st gear , and go full throttle at 1000rpms all the way to 7000rpms , the acceleration you feel ( G forces ) will be identical to the torque curve , the car will pull the hardest at peak torque and pull the least hard at wherever the least amount of torque is . So what is hp then ? HP shows the peak efficiency of the engine with gearing . When accelerating at full throttle you normally shift around peak hp because that is the peak point of efficiency that given gear has . You can bring car speed into the equation now , at a given * car speed * you will accelerate the hardest at peak power because you will be in the lowest gear possible for that speed which multiplies the most amount of engine torque .", "This is the way I got it straight in my head so it might help you too : Torque is twisting force . Consider loosening a nut with a spanner . The spanner is 1 ft long and you push with a force of 50 lb . That 's a torque of 50ftlb ( foot pounds ) Then we look at how fast you are turning the spanner . The torque multiplied by the speed ( multiplied by a constant value which I 've forgotten for now ) will give the horsepower . The special thing to remember about the torque is that if you apply the 50ftlb of torque from earlier and the nut is too tight and does n't move , then we 're still applying torque . And as the spanner is n't moving the horsepower is 50 X 0 = 0 horsepower ."]
chatgpt_answers:
["Horsepower and torque both tell you something about an engine’s ability, but they’re not the same thing.

A good way to picture horsepower is as how fast the engine can do work. It’s kind of like speed in a race: if you want to go farther, faster, more horsepower helps.

Torque is the engine’s twisting strength. Imagine trying to open a jar of pickles that’s stuck tight. The force you use to twist the lid is torque. More torque means the engine can shove harder and get things moving.

In a high-performance car, you generally want plenty of both. Horsepower helps the car keep pulling at high speed, while torque is what gives it that strong launch and makes it feel powerful when you hit the gas."]

question: How does communism exactly work ? What are the finer details about communism ? How do people receive currency ? How is the amount of currency they receive calculated ? How does the economy work and how do citizens interact with each other ? Explain like I'm five.
human_answers: ["Different kinds of communism , known as \" tendencies \" , have different views about this but most Communists follow Marx 's thoughts on \" dialectical materialism \" . That basically says that human nature is shaped by human society and so by changing society we can change human nature . And it 's not just that we can but we will , we can not not . So human nature and society is constantly changing and evolving and so the only thing we can say with confidence about future society is that it will be different to now . Communists are trying to shape this future society in the direction of communism . Communism is the idea of a moneyless , stateless , classless utopia based upon the idea of \" from each according to what they can do to each according to their need \" Some of them have attempted to answer the questions you are asking , but they are basically just guessing and you should ignore them . The real answer is that we do n't know , and we wo n't know until we get there . So we 're just going to try and shape future society in the direction of more freedom and equality and see where that gets us . Marx did make some suggestions in terms of defining principles with which to attempt to build and change a future society : - Property is theft . What this means is that no one has a moral right to the stuff that they think is theirs . If you chop a branch off a tree and use that branch to make a walking stick you might think that that is \" your \" walking stick , that you have earned . But actually you never would have been able to do so without the explorer who first scouted out the path to that tree , the blacksmith that made the axe you used to chop off the branch , the teacher who taught you how to make a walking stick , the cook that gave you the meal to give you the strength to do so , and the inventors of walking sticks , axes , paths , meals etc ... So you actually owe a debt to all these people and the walking stick is as much theirs as it is yours . And that 's a very direct example of created value . Most forms of value are even more indirect and so the amount of the value you have gained that you owe to the others who helped you get to that point is even greater . - Ownership is wage theft . Say you work in a shoe factory . You take $ 5 of material and turn them into $ 30 shoes . The owner of the shoe factory pays you $ 5 per shoe . It costs them maybe $ 5 per shoe to run the factory . The owner does nothing at all , but still makes $ 15 per shoe . But they have n't done anything for that money . That $ 15 per shoe should be yours . That is wage theft , the owner has no moral right to that money . - Rent is theft . We earn more from capital than labour . That is wrong . Thomas Picketty wrote a great book which updates this for the 21st century . Google r > g. Basically most money is n't earned by people working . It 's earned by rich people using their money to make more money without any action on their part . So say interest , shares , the value of your house going up over time , or your owning of a company which makes money : all these are examples of rent . You just sit there getting richer without doing any work . Marx says rent is theft . Picketty goes further and says the consequence of rent is that the rich will always be able to make more money without working than workers can by working , and so the gap between rich and poor is always going to grow . In addition most communists , Marx included , feel that the path to communism is socialism . Socialism is the idea that workplaces should be democratically controlled . Happy to answer any other questions you have about communism .", "Communism is , according to the original communist theorist Karl Marx , \" the real movement which abolishes the present state of things \" ( * The Germany Ideology * ) . In more concrete terms , communism is a movement that seeks the abolition of capitalism and class - society . Marx dedicated a significant portion of his work to describing what communism is and how it arises necessarily within capitalist society . Capitalism is , to Marx , a \" mode - of - production \" ( essentially ' the way production is organized within society ' ) that features generalised commodity - based production ( meaning that commodity - based production is predominant ) . Commodities are items that are both : use - values , or an item of utility for some people , and exchange - values , or an item that can be exchanged in a specific ratio for other commodities . For example : a chair is a use - value because people can sit in them ; a chair is an exchange - value because it can be exchanged in the market for other commodities like two pairs of shoes or one hundred dollars . How are the exchange - ratios of specific commodities determined -- for example , why does one chair = two pairs of shoes or ten dollars ? Marx says that exchange - values are an expression of value , and he draws the conclusion that the value of a commodity is determined by that commodity 's socially - necessary labour - time ( the average time it takes for society to produce it ) . He argues that value can not be created through exchange , but that the only source of value can be human labour . Exchange - values , or money , become what he terms ' capital ' : ' self - expanding value ' , or value that is creating more value through the only means to do so ( human labour ) . It is money ( M ) used to buy commodities ( C ) , including labour - power ( the worker 's capacity to labour ) and equipment that will create lead to the creation of a greater value of commodities ( C ' ) that can be exchanged for a greater amount of money ( M ' ) . M - C - M ' Marx illustrates that the existence of generalized commodity - production presupposes the existence of a class of people who do n't own capital and thus need to sell their capacity to labour in order to survive . The proletariat , or working - class , are restricted ownership of the ' means of production ' and forced to work for the class that does own them , the bourgeoisie or owning - class . Marx believes that the working - class is alienated and that their lives are ruled by and dependent on the existence of capital . He theorizes that it 's this relation to capital that makes them a ' revolutionary ' class , and that they have to emancipate themselves through an international revolution that abolishes nations , governments , money , and commodity - production worldwide . Your questions about currency are mistaken -- money does not exist in communism . Let me be clear , too : the USSR was not an example of communism or socialism . First : Marx never made a distinction between those two terms . Second : the existence of money and wage - labour in the USSR points to the fact that it was state - capitalist . The idea that it 's an example of communism or socialism is a Stalinist revision of Marxism to justify its state - capitalist nature . As far as what a communist world would look like : the means of production would be owned co - operatively by the entirety of society , there would be no government or state to enforce codified laws , people would choose to work , production would not be for the creation of surplus - value but for the needs of society , and its products would be freely distributed based on the principle : \" from each according to his ability , to each according to his needs \" . It is , in a sense , the beginning of history .", "The reality is , communism * does n't * work . The other comments give a good description of the * idea * of communism . Sure we might believe that the * idea * of communism is good , but how do we put it into practice ? That is the problem . It has been tried ( in the USSR , in China , etc ) but every attempt so far has involved serious abuses of human rights ."]

question: Why does the earth 's rotation affect a pendulum , but not anything else that hovers above the ground ? If the earth is truly rotating , atmosphere and all , then the Foucault pendulum would n't be possible whatsoever , would it ? Explain like I'm five.
human_answers: ["The * Coriolis effect * happens when you 're on a rotating surface and you change your distance from the center of rotation . It 's easiest to explain if you think about standing on a merry - go - round . All the horses on the merry - go - round make one full rotation in the same amount of time . But the horses farther from the center have a longer distance to travel in that same amount of time . So they 're moving faster than the horses near the center . What if you try to jump from one horse to another ? If you 're on a horse near the center and you jump outwards , you 're jumping from a slower horse to a faster horse . As you jump outwards , you 're moving with the same speed as the slower horse was ( because of inertia ) , but suddenly you 're in a zone of faster horses . The horse you were trying to jump onto is moving faster than you are , so it outpaces you and you fall on the floor behind it . In other words , moving outwards from the center of rotation causes you to drift backwards , from the point of view of the rotating merry - go - round . The opposite happens if you jump from one of the outer horses inwards - you 're moving faster than the horse you 're aiming at , so you drift forwards . The same thing happens on Earth 's surface . The North and South Poles are at the axis of rotation , so they have no lateral speed . But the Equator is moving at over 1000 mph . So as you move from one of the Poles toward the Equator , you will drift \" backwards \" with respect to Earth 's rotation ( i.e. westward ) . If you 're moving from the Equator toward one of the Poles , you 'll drift \" forwards \" ( eastward ) . This affects any floating object to some extent . But it 's a very small effect - you only see it with things that are really really big ( like ocean currents ) or really really fast ( like long - range artillery ) .", "Well , it does . It is known as the coriolis effect . The Foucault pendulum is just a way to show that the earth is rotating by being able to measure the changes in the path of the pendulum as the earth rotates .", "It 's not so much that the earth 's rotation has an effect on the pendulum , it 's actually that it * does n't * . What 's really happening is that the pendulum keeps moving in the same direction , but the earth is rotating underneath it . There is an animation [ here ] ( URL_0 ) that shows what 's really going on ."]
chatgpt_answers:
["Earth’s rotation does affect everything near the surface, including things that are floating in the air. It’s just that, in most cases, the effect is so small that you can’t easily see it.

For example, if you drop a ball, gravity pulls it downward, and Earth’s spin only nudges its path a tiny bit. Since the ball is small and air drag is involved, that rotation effect is usually too slight to notice.

A pendulum is different. It’s a long, heavy object that keeps moving back and forth for a while, and its motion is only gradually reduced by air resistance and other small losses. Because it swings for a long time, the fact that the ground underneath it is turning with Earth becomes visible.

So the pendulum appears to change direction because it is swinging relative to the ground, while the ground itself is rotating. That is why Earth’s rotation is easy to see in a Foucault pendulum, even though the same rotation is much less obvious in most other objects hovering above the ground."]

question: Why is there so much smoke produced after a fire goes out ? Even if the material seems to burn clean before it goes out . Explain like I'm five.
